fact: November 15 is exactly 319 days into the year, which means there are only 46 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for squirrels to go absolutely nuts burying acorns - they can bury up to 10,000 nuts and remember where 90% of them are hidden!
The sun sets before 5 PM in most northern cities around this date, making it feel like bedtime when you're barely done with homework.
November 15 falls smack in the middle of Scorpio season, when people born under this zodiac sign are said to be extra mysterious and intense.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, November 15 is when jacaranda trees explode with bright purple flowers that carpet entire streets in violet petals.
This date sits exactly 54 days after the autumn equinox in the north, meaning winter is charging toward us like a freight train.
Deer in North America are in full rutting season around November 15, which means male deer are acting like total show-offs, fighting and making weird grunting noises to impress females.
The moon phase on November 15 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it's called the Frost Moon or Beaver Moon by some Native American tribes.
In northern regions, lakes and ponds start forming their first thin sheets of ice around this time, creating nature's own skating rinks that crack and sing in the cold.
November 15 marks the peak time for maple trees to drop their last stubborn leaves, creating crunchy leaf piles perfect for jumping into.
The Northern Lights become more visible around this date because the nights are getting longer and darker, giving the aurora borealis a better stage to dance across the sky.
In many places, November 15 brings the first real frost that kills off summer bugs - it's like nature's pest control finally kicking in.
